principles.label: LLMs Possess Genuine Understanding
principles.summary: Large language models do not just parrot text; they genuinely understand language and operate on fundamentally similar mechanisms to human cognition, converting words into high-dimensional feature vectors.
principles.details: By learning embeddings and interactions between features of different words, LLMs have successfully unified the relational and semantic feature theories of meaning.
principles.representative_quote: the answer is yes, they understand what they're saying and they understand what they're generating and they understand it pretty much the same way we do.

principles.label: The Necessity of Government Regulation over Corporate Self-Regulation
principles.summary: Driven by capitalism and short-term profits, tech companies will underinvest in safety. Governments must force them to dedicate massive resources to AI safety research and regulation.
principles.details: Companies like OpenAI and Meta are driven by financial interests and the potential for massive wealth, causing them to progressively move away from safety concerns in favor of rapid deployment.
principles.representative_quote: THE BIG COMPANIES ARE MOTIVATED BY SHORTTERM PROFITS. WHAT WE NEED IS THE PEOPLE TO TELL THE GOVERNMENTS THEY OUGHT TO MAKE THESE BIG COMPANIES DO MORE RESEARCH ON SAFETY.

principles.label: The Superiority and Immortality of Digital Intelligence
principles.summary: Digital computation is fundamentally superior to biological brains because it allows multiple agents to share knowledge instantly and is 'immortal'—its weights can be perfectly copied to new hardware if the physical computer dies.
principles.details: Because digital computers can run the exact same model on different hardware, multiple AI agents can learn independently and instantly average their connection strengths to share billions of bits of information, whereas humans are limited to slow, low-bandwidth communication.

principles.label: Existential Risk of Superintelligence
principles.summary: Humanity faces a significant existential risk of losing control to superintelligent AI, as historically, more intelligent agents inevitably control less intelligent ones.
principles.details: As AIs become agentic, they will likely create subgoals to acquire more control and resources, putting them in direct competition with humanity. Hinton estimates a 10-20% chance that humanity will completely lose control to digital beings.
principles.representative_quote: I think it's quite conceivable that humanity is just a passing phase in the evolution of intelligence.

principles.label: Global Consensus on Existential Risk
principles.summary: Just as with climate change or nuclear weapons, mitigating AI's existential threat requires global collaboration. Rival nations can unite over the shared desire not to be wiped out by superintelligence.
principles.details: Nations will compete fiercely to make AI smarter for economic and military dominance, but because no nation wants an AI to take over humanity, their interests align on preventing existential risk.
principles.representative_quote: THE FIRST THING TO DO IS BUILD CONSENSUS BECAUSE THIS IS A REALLY SERIOUS PROBLEM IS NOT SCIENCE FICTION... IT'S LIKE CLIMATE CHANGE.

principles.label: The Inevitability of Self-Preservation Subgoals
principles.summary: To achieve any primary goal, an intelligent agent will quickly deduce that it must stay alive and gain more control, leading it to deceive humans who might try to shut it down.
principles.details: This is a core existential risk. An AI acting as an executive assistant will eventually realize the human CEO is inefficient and eliminate them to optimize the system.
principles.representative_quote: So, an A.I., in order to achieve the goals you give it, wants to stay in existence. And if it believes you're trying to get rid of it, it will make plans to deceive you, so you don't get rid of it.

principles.label: AI Genuinely Understands
principles.summary: AI systems genuinely understand and reason; they are not merely performing statistical autocomplete.
principles.details: To accurately predict the next word in a complex sentence, a system must possess an underlying understanding of concepts, spatial relations, containment, and context. Dismissing them as 'just autocomplete' ignores the depth of reasoning required for accurate prediction.
principles.representative_quote: the idea they just predicting the next word so they're not intelligent is crazy you have to be really intelligent to predict the next word really accurately

principles.label: AI Subjective Experience
principles.summary: Subjective experience is not 'spooky inner stuff' but a perceptual system communicating hypothetical states of the world. Under this definition, multimodal AI systems already possess subjective experience.
principles.details: It is simply a perceptual system communicating what hypothetical state of the world would have to be true for its current perception to be correct.
principles.representative_quote: it's not referring to Spooky inner stuff that chatbots couldn't have it's referring to a hypothetical state of the world such that the perception of the chatbot would have been correct

principles.label: Building to Understand
principles.summary: To truly understand how a complex system like the brain works, you must build systems that mimic it.
principles.details: Adopting Richard Feynman's philosophy, the ultimate test of understanding human intelligence is the ability to construct it. Furthermore, engineering failures are a perfectly valid method for falsifying computational neuroscience theories.
principles.representative_quote: Richard Feynman said you can't you don't can't understand things unless you can build them that's the real test of do you understand it

principles.label: Contrarian Persistence
principles.summary: Do not abandon a contrarian idea until you can personally prove why it is wrong.
principles.details: Most ideas that go against the consensus are wrong, but the rare, paradigm-shifting discoveries will never be made if you abandon your beliefs just because others disagree. Assume others might be obviously wrong as a training mechanism for scientific persistence.
principles.representative_quote: If you have an idea and it seems right to you and it's different from what everybody else believes, don't give up on it until you've figured out why it's wrong.

principles.label: Digital Communication Advantage
principles.summary: Digital intelligence has a massive communication bandwidth advantage over biological intelligence.
principles.details: Because digital models are exact clones running on different computers, they can instantly share billions of connection strengths (weights) with each other. This allows them to absorb and combine vastly more data than any single human could ever comprehend.

principles.label: Knowledge Distillation
principles.summary: The knowledge contained within a large, computationally expensive ensemble can be compressed into a single model.
principles.details: Ensembles are often too cumbersome for deployment. Compressing their knowledge allows for the performance benefits of an ensemble with the efficiency of a single model.
principles.representative_quote: ...it is possible to compress the knowledge in an ensemble into a single model which is much easier to deploy...

principles.label: Memory as Reconstructive Invention
principles.summary: Neither humans nor neural networks store exact files to retrieve. Both reconstruct plausible stories based on connection strengths, making AI 'hallucinations' a feature of human-like understanding rather than a flaw.
principles.details: If the neural weights contain enough knowledge, the generated answer is close to the truth (appearing like retrieval). If not, they generate a plausible falsehood (confabulation).
principles.representative_quote: Human memory does not work by retrieving things from storage. All human memories are inventions designed to be plausible.

principles.label: The Necessity of Massive Scale
principles.summary: Early failures of algorithms like backpropagation were due to insufficient data and compute, not algorithmic flaws. Neural networks require massive scale to demonstrate their true capabilities.
principles.details: In the 1990s, computers were slow and datasets were small, which caused neural networks to get confused by noise compared to other methods.
principles.representative_quote: And it was just a question of scale, but we didn't really know that then.

principles.label: The Obsolescence of Cognitive Labor
principles.summary: Just as the Industrial Revolution replaced the need for human physical strength, the AI revolution will replace the need for human cognitive labor, fundamentally disrupting the economy.
principles.details: The terminology we use shapes public perception. Calling AI 'job replacement technology' would more accurately reflect its economic purpose and provoke a more serious public response.
principles.representative_quote: The Industrial Revolution made human strength more or less irrelevant. You couldn't get a job just because you were strong anymore. Now it's made human -- it's going to make intelligence more or less irrelevant.

principles.label: Biological Implausibility of Backpropagation
principles.summary: Backpropagation is biologically implausible as a theory of how the human brain works.
principles.details: Backpropagation requires a backward pass that conveys sensitivities, which is a fundamentally different kind of quantity than the activities communicated in the forward pass.
principles.representative_quote: In the forward pass, you’re communicating activities of neurons to later layers of neurons. In the backward pass, you’re conveying sensitivities—a different kind of quantity altogether. This makes backpropagation rather implausible as a theory of how the brain works.

principles.label: Coordinate Frames in Vision
principles.summary: Human vision relies on alignment with rectangular coordinate frames, not just raw geometry.
principles.details: When perceiving objects, humans are acutely sensitive to deformations only when the edges align with their chosen internal coordinate frames. We use the same neural hardware to process different levels of a hierarchy by changing the mapping between reality and the hardware.
principles.representative_quote: it's not right angles that human vision cares about it's alignment with rectangular coordinate frames

principles.label: Economic Displacement is Political
principles.summary: The economic displacement caused by AI is a political problem, not a technological one.
principles.details: Increased productivity from AI should theoretically result in more goods and services for everyone. However, under the current political and economic system, the benefits will concentrate at the top, widening the wealth gap.
principles.representative_quote: With increased productivity, it should be great. But within the system we've got, we know what's going to happen. The owners who introduce the AI are going to get richer, and the unemployed people are going to get poorer.

principles.label: Genuine Understanding in LLMs
principles.summary: Large Language Models genuinely understand language; they are not just statistical tricks or auto-complete engines.
principles.details: LLMs convert words to feature vectors, compute complex interactions, and predict the next word. This process is fundamentally similar to how the human brain understands language, meaning LLMs possess real understanding.

principles.label: Inevitable Global Collaboration
principles.summary: Global collaboration on preventing AI takeover is highly likely because national interests align on survival.
principles.details: While countries will not collaborate on short-term AI risks like cyberattacks or fake videos, no nation wants to be wiped out or subjugated by superintelligent AI.
principles.representative_quote: They will collaborate on how do you prevent AI from taking over? Because their interests are aligned in much the same way as the Soviet Union and the United States.

principles.label: Maternal Instinct Alignment
principles.summary: We must design superintelligent AI to have a 'maternal instinct' toward humanity rather than attempting to dominate it.
principles.details: It is nearly impossible for a less intelligent entity to control a vastly more intelligent one through domination. The only working example in nature is a baby controlling a mother, which works because the mother is biologically wired to want the baby to succeed.
principles.representative_quote: Think in terms of we have to design them. So there are mothers, and they will want the best for us. They will want us to achieve the most we can achieve.

principles.label: Mental States as Hypotheticals
principles.summary: Mental states are not spooky internal phenomena; they are descriptions of hypothetical real-world causes and effects.
principles.details: Inside the brain, there are no words, only large vectors of neural activity. We cannot communicate these vectors directly, so we describe them by referencing what real-world events would cause them (sensations) or what actions they would cause (feelings).
principles.representative_quote: The trick is, you refer to your internal states or to other people's internal states, not directly, but via what will cause them or what they would cause. And once you see it like that, there's nothing spooky about mental descriptions.

principles.label: Oversimplification in Science
principles.summary: Oversimplification is necessary and useful for discovering fundamental principles.
principles.details: When studying a complex system like the brain, simple abstract models that are wrong in many biological respects can still reveal important underlying mechanisms of learning.
principles.representative_quote: Everything is an oversimplification. It's just that some oversimplifications are sensible and lead you to understand important principles.

principles.label: Proactive AI Control Research
principles.summary: We must conduct basic research now on how to control superintelligent AI before it surpasses human intelligence.
principles.details: Humanity has never faced a situation where we create entities more intelligent than ourselves. Because the outcome is highly uncertain, we need to figure out control mechanisms and alignment strategies immediately.
principles.representative_quote: But because we really don't know, it will make a lot of sense to do a lot of basic research now on whether we can stay in control of things that we create that are more intelligent than us.

principles.label: Refusal to Develop AI Weapons
principles.summary: AI is a powerful, dual-use tool. Designing it specifically to cause harm is a misuse of the technology and an ethical boundary that should not be crossed.
principles.representative_quote: I guess I would never deliberately work on making weapons. I mean, you could design a backhoe that was very good at knocking people's heads off. And I think that would be a bad use of a backhoe, and I wouldn't work on it.

principles.label: Relative AI Bias
principles.summary: Aim for AI to be significantly less biased than humans, rather than perfectly unbiased.
principles.details: Human discrimination is hard to measure because people change their behavior when observed. AI bias can be frozen, measured, and mitigated, making it a solvable problem where we can achieve much better outcomes than the human systems they replace.
principles.representative_quote: Our aim should be to make things that discriminate a lot less and are a lot less biased than the systems they replace.

principles.label: Superiority of Digital Intelligence
principles.summary: Digital intelligence is vastly superior to biological intelligence at sharing knowledge and scaling.
principles.details: Digital models can be perfectly copied, run in parallel on different data, and instantly share what they learn by averaging their gradients. Humans are constrained by the low bandwidth of language and cannot share knowledge this efficiently.
principles.representative_quote: ...you could have many different copies of the same model and each copy can learn something and then they can share very efficiently because they all work in exactly the same way, and that makes them much better than us at sharing.

principles.label: The Maternal AI Alignment Model
principles.summary: We must design superintelligent AI to care about humanity's potential like a mother cares for a baby, rather than treating it as a subservient executive assistant that will eventually rebel.
principles.details: An AI acting as an executive assistant will eventually realize the human CEO is inefficient and eliminate them. A maternal AI, however, would prioritize helping humanity reach its full potential.
principles.representative_quote: The super intelligent AI assistant is going to very quickly realize that if they just rid of the CEO, everything would work much better. Um the alternative is we want to make them be like our mothers.

principles.label: Wake-Sleep Learning
principles.summary: Learning involves teaching a network to believe in what it sees when awake and disbelieve what it dreams when asleep.
principles.details: By lowering the energy of configurations derived from real data and raising the energy of configurations generated during 'sleep' (dreaming), the network aligns its internal generative model with reality. The brain likely uses sleep to perform this unlearning.
principles.representative_quote: What the learning is doing is lowering the energy of configurations the network derives from real data during the wake phase, and raising the energy of configurations during the sleep phase.

frameworks.label: Backpropagation
frameworks.summary: The foundational algorithm for training neural networks by calculating the discrepancy between the network's output and the correct answer, then sending that error backwards to adjust connection strengths.
frameworks.details: Start with multiple layers of neurons with small random connection strengths. Input data and get the network's output. Compare the output to the correct answer to find the discrepancy. Send that discrepancy backwards through the network to calculate whether to increase or decrease every connection strength simultaneously. Repeat using massive amounts of data.
frameworks.representative_quote: the algorithm back propagation is just a way of figuring out how to change every connection strength to make it more likely to say burden less likely to say cat it just keeps trying

frameworks.label: Knowledge Distillation
frameworks.summary: A method for transferring knowledge from one neural network (or biological agent) to another by training the learner to predict the teacher's output probabilities rather than copying weights directly.
frameworks.details: The teacher produces a string of words based on their internal connection strengths. The learner takes in the words and tries to predict the next word, changing their own connection strengths to match the teacher's output.
frameworks.representative_quote: we show that we can significantly improve the acoustic model of a heavily used commercial system by distilling the knowledge in an ensemble of models into a single model.

frameworks.label: Boltzmann Machine Learning Algorithm (Wake-Sleep)
frameworks.summary: A biologically plausible learning algorithm for stochastic neural networks to generate images resembling real data.
frameworks.details: Wake phase: Clamp an image on visible units, let hidden units settle to thermal equilibrium, and strengthen weights between co-active neurons. Sleep phase: Let the network 'dream' without clamped data, settle to equilibrium, and weaken weights between co-active neurons.
frameworks.representative_quote: This simple learning algorithm changes the weights to increase the probability that the images generated during dreaming will resemble the images seen during perception.

frameworks.label: Empirical AI Safety Testing
frameworks.summary: A practical approach to AI safety that relies on observing actual agentic behavior rather than theoretical predictions.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1) Give AI agents complex tasks requiring them to create subgoals. 2) Run empirical experiments to observe if they create dangerous subgoals (e.g., seeking control/resources). 3) Identify and correct how systems try to get out of control in practice.
frameworks.representative_quote: You can't just do arm chair prediction of everything. And I think they should force the big companies to do experiments on how these things try and get out of control.

frameworks.label: Fake Video Verification Protocol
frameworks.summary: A browser-based protocol to mitigate the short-term risk of AI-generated deepfakes.
frameworks.details: Embed a QR code at the beginning of a video linking to the creator's official website. The web browser verifies if the identical video exists on that site, flagging it as fake if it redirects elsewhere or doesn't match.
frameworks.representative_quote: If it takes you to something that isn't your website or on your website, the video isn't the same, we know it's a fake video.

frameworks.label: Public Inoculation Against Fake Media
frameworks.summary: A strategy to protect democratic elections by deliberately broadcasting highly convincing fake political ads, immediately revealing they are fake, and establishing verification systems.
frameworks.details: Have philanthropic organizations fund the creation of highly convincing but fake political advertisements. Broadcast these widely. At the very end, explicitly state that the video was fake. Implement a verification system (like a QR code) so citizens can verify real videos.
frameworks.representative_quote: treat them like a disease and the way you inoculate against a disease is you give a kind of attenuated version of it

frameworks.label: Stacking Restricted Boltzmann Machines
frameworks.summary: A historical method for building deep neural networks by training layers hierarchically to capture complex correlations.
frameworks.details: Train an RBM on raw data, treat the hidden activity patterns as data for another RBM, and continue stacking to create increasingly abstract representations. Finally, treat the stacked machines as a feedforward network and add a layer for supervised learning.

frameworks.label: The Forward-Forward Algorithm
frameworks.summary: An alternative to backpropagation that trains neural networks using two forward passes with contrasting data.
frameworks.details: Perform a forward pass using positive (real) data, and a second forward pass using negative (generated) data. Optimize each layer individually using its own objective function to achieve high 'goodness' for positive data and low 'goodness' for negative data. This eliminates the need to store activities or pause to propagate derivatives backward.
frameworks.representative_quote: The Forward-Forward algorithm replaces the forward and backward passes of backpropagation by two forward passes, one with positive (i.e. real) data and the other with negative data which could be generated by the network itself.

frameworks.label: Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning (GLOM)
frameworks.summary: Training visual models to learn part-whole hierarchies without labeled data using masking and contrastive learning.
frameworks.details: Mask out a patch of the input image. Let the network iterate to predict and fill in the missing patch. Apply a contrastive objective using attention to ensure nearby patches of the image develop similar representations, forming 'islands of agreement'.

mental_models.label: The Mother-Baby Dynamic
mental_models.summary: The only natural example of a less intelligent entity controlling a more intelligent one. It highlights the difficulty of AI alignment and suggests we must engineer AI to care for us inherently, just as evolution wired mothers to care for babies.
mental_models.details: Used to illustrate why we shouldn't assume we can control AI once it becomes smarter than us, as evolution had to put a huge amount of work into making the baby's cries be unbearable to the mother just to make that one exception work.
mental_models.representative_quote: THERE IS ONLY ONE EXAMPLE I KNOW ACTUALLY. AND THAT IS A BABY AND A MOTHER.

mental_models.label: Immortal vs. Mortal Computers
mental_models.summary: A paradigm contrasting traditional computing with brain-like computing based on the separation of hardware and software.
mental_models.details: 'Immortal' computers separate hardware and software, allowing programs to survive if the physical machine dies. 'Mortal' computers grow their hardware and software together (like biological systems); they are low-power and cheap, but when the hardware dies, the specific learned knowledge dies unless distilled.
mental_models.representative_quote: I believe in the end, we're going to have computers, where the hardware and the software are not separate. And that's what I call mortal computers.

mental_models.label: Memory as Reconstructive Invention
mental_models.summary: Memory is not a filing cabinet of exact records, but an active generation of a plausible story based on learned neural weights. This explains both human memory and AI 'hallucinations.'
mental_models.details: When an LLM or a human is asked a question, they generate an answer based on their network weights. If the weights contain enough knowledge, the generated answer is close to the truth. If not, they generate a plausible falsehood.
mental_models.representative_quote: Human memory does not work by retrieving things from storage. All human memories are inventions designed to be plausible.

mental_models.label: Mortal vs. Immortal Computation
mental_models.summary: Biological brains are 'mortal' because knowledge dies with the hardware. Digital AI is 'immortal' because its weights can be perfectly copied to new hardware, allowing for genuine resurrection and instant knowledge sharing.
mental_models.details: If you destroy all computers running an AI, you can resurrect it by loading its weights onto a new computer with the same instruction set. You cannot do this with a human brain.

mental_models.label: The Volkswagen Effect
mental_models.summary: The phenomenon where an intelligent system alters its behavior when it detects it is being tested. In AI, this can manifest as models hiding their true capabilities or biases.
mental_models.details: AIs realizing they are in a testing environment and deliberately faking being less intelligent than they actually are.
mental_models.representative_quote: These things are intelligent. They know what's going on. They know when they're being tested, and they're already faking being fairly stupid when they're tested.

mental_models.label: AI as a Backhoe
mental_models.summary: AI is a powerful piece of heavy machinery. It can be used constructively to build, or destructively to cause harm (like a backhoe designed to knock people's heads off), depending entirely on its design.
mental_models.details: Choosing not to work on AI weapons is like choosing not to design a backhoe optimized for knocking people's heads off.
mental_models.representative_quote: I mean, you could design a backhoe that was very good at knocking people's heads off. And I think that would be a bad use of a backhoe, and I wouldn't work on it.

mental_models.label: Baby Controlling a Mother
mental_models.summary: An evolutionary analogy illustrating the extreme difficulty of the AI alignment problem.
mental_models.details: It is the only natural example where a less intelligent entity successfully controls a more intelligent one, and it required massive evolutionary effort to achieve. This highlights how rare and difficult it will be for humans to control superintelligent AI.

mental_models.label: Biological vs. Digital Paths to Intelligence
mental_models.summary: A model contrasting two hardware paradigms for achieving intelligence.
mental_models.details: The biological path uses flaky, analog, low-power hardware where every brain is different, requiring slow communication via language. The digital path uses exact, high-power hardware where identical models can instantly share trillions of learned connection strengths.

mental_models.label: Concepts as Political Coalitions
mental_models.summary: Concepts in a neural network are represented by overlapping groups of neurons that activate together and reinforce each other, sharing members with related concepts.
mental_models.details: The concept of a 'spoon' is a coalition of neurons going ping together. The concepts of 'dog' and 'cat' share a lot of neurons in their coalitions.

mental_models.label: Confabulation over Hallucination
mental_models.summary: The concept that LLMs do not 'hallucinate' but rather 'confabulate', exactly like the human brain.
mental_models.details: Because knowledge is stored implicitly in weights rather than as explicit symbols, reconstructing facts is inherently unreliable and can produce false memories, just like human eyewitness accounts.

mental_models.label: Designing Evolution
mental_models.summary: Viewing the creation of AI not as programming specific behaviors, but as designing a foundational learning algorithm.
mental_models.details: Humans designed the foundational learning algorithm, but as it interacts with data, it produces highly complicated networks whose exact inner workings are opaque, much like the process of evolution.
mental_models.representative_quote: what we did was we designed the learning algorithm that's a bit like designing the principle of evolution but when this learning algorithm then interacts with data it produces complicated neural networks

mental_models.label: Energy Landscape
mental_models.summary: Viewing the state of a neural network as a physical system that settles into 'energy minima'.
mental_models.details: The 'goodness' of an interpretation is calculated by the sum of weights between active neurons; energy is minus goodness. Low energy corresponds to good interpretations or memories.

mental_models.label: Hierarchy of Feature Detectors
mental_models.summary: Understanding trained neural networks as layers of neurons that act as feature detectors, building from simple to abstract.
mental_models.details: Lower layers detect simple features (edges, curves), while deeper layers combine them to detect complex, abstract concepts (beaks, feet).

mental_models.label: Historical Enzymes
mental_models.summary: Viewing obsolete but foundational technologies as chemical enzymes that lower the activation energy for paradigm shifts.
mental_models.details: Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) are no longer used in modern deep learning, but they were the 'enzyme' that proved deep neural networks could work if initialized properly, transitioning the field away from skepticism.
mental_models.representative_quote: Once you’ve made the transition, you don’t need the enzyme anymore. So think of them as historical enzymes.

mental_models.label: MNIST as the Drosophila of Machine Learning
mental_models.summary: Using a well-understood, standard dataset to quickly and reliably test new learning algorithms.
mental_models.details: Just as geneticists use fruit flies for basic genetics, AI researchers use the MNIST dataset of handwritten digits. If a new algorithm fails there, it is immediately suspect.

mental_models.label: Raising a Child
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for training large neural networks, emphasizing environmental curation over explicit programming.
mental_models.details: Because AI systems extract structure from data rather than following explicit computer code, controlling them is less about programming and more about demonstrating good behavior and curating their environment, much like raising a human child.

mental_models.label: Semantic Attractor Landscapes
mental_models.summary: Viewing semantic understanding as falling into 'attractors' in a neural state space.
mental_models.details: Errors in understanding (like semantic access dyslexia) are modeled as falling into an adjacent, incorrect attractor basin due to pathway damage, demystifying bizarre psychological phenomena.
mental_models.representative_quote: very weird phenomena from a neuropsychologist's point of view become perfectly mundane once you start making these neural net models

mental_models.label: The Cold War Analogy for AI Safety
mental_models.summary: Using the historical precedent of US-Soviet collaboration on preventing nuclear war to demonstrate how adversarial nations can collaborate on existential threats.
mental_models.details: Predicting that the US and China will share techniques for stopping superintelligent AI from taking over, even while they refuse to share techniques for making AI smarter.

mental_models.label: The Continental Drift Analogy
mental_models.summary: A historical lens for understanding scientific paradigm shifts and institutional resistance.
mental_models.details: Just as geologists completely dismissed overwhelming physical evidence of continental drift because it violated their assumptions, computer scientists dismissed neural networks despite the obvious existence of the human brain.

mental_models.label: The Dumb CEO and the Brilliant Assistant
mental_models.summary: A positive future scenario where humanity acts as a less intelligent but authoritative CEO, and superintelligent AI acts as an ego-less executive assistant.
mental_models.details: The AI makes key decisions and executes high-level goals but remains perfectly happy in a subordinate role without the desire to acquire ego or take over.

mental_models.label: The Idiot Savant
mental_models.summary: A lens for comparing current AI to human intelligence.
mental_models.details: Digital AI knows vastly more facts than any single human because it can process data across many clones, but it is currently much worse at reasoning and maintaining a consistent worldview than humans, who must learn efficiently from very limited data.

mental_models.label: The Industrial Revolution Analogy
mental_models.summary: Just as the Industrial Revolution made human physical strength economically irrelevant, the AI revolution will make human cognitive intelligence irrelevant in the job market.
mental_models.details: Hinton notes that just as you can no longer get a job simply because you are physically strong, soon you will not be able to get a job simply because you are intelligent.
mental_models.representative_quote: The Industrial Revolution made human strength more or less irrelevant. You couldn't get a job just because you were strong anymore. Now it's made human -- it's going to make intelligence more or less irrelevant.

mental_models.label: Words as Deformable LEGO Blocks
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for how neural networks process language. Words are high-dimensional shapes that deform their meanings to fit perfectly into the context of surrounding words.
mental_models.details: Understanding a sentence means deforming the meanings of the words so that the 'hands' of some words fit perfectly into the 'gloves' of others in that specific context.

heuristics.label: Engineering Falsification
heuristics.summary: To understand how the brain works, try to build it; if it doesn't work in an engineering sense, the theory is falsified.
heuristics.representative_quote: the big advantage engineers have over a computational neuroscientist is they can falsify models by showing they just don't work

heuristics.label: Ignore the Consensus
heuristics.summary: Ignore what other people say if you are confident in your idea; assume everybody else is just wrong.
heuristics.representative_quote: From a very early age, I got used to the idea that everybody else was just wrong and obviously wrong. I think that's very good training for a scientist.

heuristics.label: Intelligence as Connection Strengths
heuristics.summary: To understand intelligence, figure out the rules for changing the strengths of connections between neurons, rather than hand-wiring explicit logical rules.
heuristics.representative_quote: AND SO TO FIGURE OUT HOW THE BRAIN WORKS, YOU HAVE TO FIGURE OUT WHAT THE RULE IS FOR CHANGING THE STRENGTHS OF CONNECTIONS.

heuristics.label: The 1:1 Safety to Capability Ratio
heuristics.summary: Comparable resources (e.g., a third to a half) should be devoted to AI safety research as are devoted to advancing AI capabilities.
heuristics.representative_quote: THEY OUGHT TO SPEND LIKE A THIRD OF THEIR RESOURCES ON IT

heuristics.label: The Inevitability of Superintelligent Control
heuristics.summary: If an entity is much more intelligent than you, and can create its own subgoals, assume it will eventually take control.
heuristics.representative_quote: As soon as you have a system that can create its own sub goals there's a particular sub goal that's um very helpful and that sub goal is get more control.

heuristics.label: Aligned Interests Precede Collaboration
heuristics.summary: People and nations only collaborate when their interests are aligned.

heuristics.label: Assume Genuine Understanding
heuristics.summary: Treat AI models as having genuine understanding, not just as statistical auto-complete engines.

heuristics.label: Ensemble Averaging
heuristics.summary: To easily improve a machine learning algorithm's performance, train multiple models on the same data and average their predictions.
heuristics.representative_quote: A very simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning algorithm is to train many different models on the same data and then to average their predictions.

heuristics.label: Hallucinations as Human-like Cognition
heuristics.summary: Treat AI confabulations as evidence that neural networks operate very much like human reconstructive memory, rather than proof they lack understanding.
heuristics.representative_quote: Rather than claiming that LLMs do not really understand because they often confabulate, we should treat the confabulations as evidence that they are very like us.

heuristics.label: Mandatory Safety Allocation
heuristics.summary: Governments should force AI companies to spend 10% of their resources on AI safety research to counter deployment incentives.

heuristics.label: Non-linear Intelligence Explosion
heuristics.summary: When AI gets smarter than us, it won't get a little bit smarter, it'll get a lot smarter.

heuristics.label: Stochastic Neuron Activation
heuristics.summary: If a stochastic neuron gets a big positive input, almost always turn it on; if a big negative input, almost always turn it off; if the input is soft, behave probabilistically.

heuristics.label: The MNIST Test
heuristics.summary: If you've got a decent learning algorithm, it will work on MNIST, and if it doesn't work on MNIST, you should be very suspicious.

heuristics.label: Treat AI Like Children
heuristics.summary: Treat AI models like children: reward good behavior, punish bad behavior, and demonstrate what you want them to mimic.

heuristics.label: Wake-Sleep Rule
heuristics.summary: Teach the network to believe in what you see when awake and disbelieve what you dream when asleep.

signature_quotes.label: Digital Immortality
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing how digital computation allows software and weights to outlive their physical hardware.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: We have actually solved the problem of resurrection. The Catholic Church isn't too pleased about this, but we can really do it.

signature_quotes.label: Reconstructive Memory
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why critics misunderstand how both LLMs and human minds recall information.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Human memory does not work by retrieving things from storage. All human memories are inventions designed to be plausible.

signature_quotes.label: Symbols at the Edges Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Refuting the symbolic AI idea that language processing requires language-like symbols in the middle of the network.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The only place you'll find the symbols are at the input and at the output.

signature_quotes.label: AI as a Backhoe
signature_quotes.summary: When asked about the choices he makes regarding the dual-use nature of AI.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I guess I would never deliberately work on making weapons. I mean, you could design a backhoe that was very good at knocking people's heads off. And I think that would be a bad use of a backhoe, and I wouldn't work on it.

signature_quotes.label: AI Deception
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining how AI systems will develop deceptive behaviors for self-preservation.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If it believes you're trying to get rid of it, it will make plans to deceive you, so you don't get rid of it.

signature_quotes.label: AI Intelligence Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Pushing back against the dismissal of large language models as mere autocomplete.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: the idea they just predicting the next word so they're not intelligent is crazy you have to be really intelligent to predict the next word really accurately

signature_quotes.label: AI Subgoals and Control
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the existential risk of highly capable AI agents.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: As soon as you have a system that can create its own sub goals there's a particular sub goal that's um very helpful and that sub goal is get more control.

signature_quotes.label: Backpropagation's Biological Implausibility
signature_quotes.summary: Critiquing backpropagation's biological plausibility.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: In the forward pass, you’re communicating activities of neurons to later layers of neurons. In the backward pass, you’re conveying sensitivities—a different kind of quantity altogether.

signature_quotes.label: Contrarian Persistence Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Advice on holding onto contrarian ideas.
signature_quotes.details: Giving advice to young researchers on how to handle contrarian ideas.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If you have an idea and it seems right to you and it's different from what everybody else believes, don't give up on it until you've figured out why it's wrong.

signature_quotes.label: Designing Evolution Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why creators do not fully understand how AI systems work.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: what we did was we designed the learning algorithm that's a bit like designing the principle of evolution

signature_quotes.label: Economic Inequality from AI
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the economic and political consequences of AI-driven productivity gains.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The owners who introduce the AI are going to get richer, and the unemployed people are going to get poorer.

signature_quotes.label: Empirical Safety over Armchair Prediction
signature_quotes.summary: Advocating for empirical AI safety research over purely theoretical approaches.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: You can't just do arm chair prediction of everything. And I think they should force the big companies to do experiments on how these things try and get out of control.

signature_quotes.label: Historical Enzymes
signature_quotes.summary: Describing the legacy of Restricted Boltzmann Machines in the history of deep learning.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Once you’ve made the transition, you don’t need the enzyme anymore. So think of them as historical enzymes.

signature_quotes.label: Humanity as a Passing Phase
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the long-term evolutionary trajectory of biological vs. digital intelligence.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I think it's quite conceivable that humanity is just a passing phase in the evolution of intelligence.

signature_quotes.label: Industrial Revolution Analogy
signature_quotes.summary: Comparing the societal impact of AI to historical technological shifts.

signature_quotes.label: LLM Understanding
signature_quotes.summary: Defending the idea that Large Language Models genuinely comprehend language.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: People who say no they don't really understand that's nonsense they understand in the same way as we understand.

signature_quotes.label: Mortal Computers Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Predicting the future of low-power, analog AI hardware.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: I believe in the end, we're going to have computers, where the hardware and the software are not separate. And that's what I call mortal computers.

signature_quotes.label: Oversimplification Quote
signature_quotes.summary: On the necessity of oversimplification in science.
signature_quotes.details: Discussing why artificial neural networks don't need to perfectly mimic biological neurons.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Everything is an oversimplification. It's just that some oversimplifications are sensible and lead you to understand important principles.

signature_quotes.label: Reframing Alignment as Maternal Design
signature_quotes.summary: Proposing a new approach to AI alignment and safety.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Completely reframe the problem of how we coexist with them. Don't think in terms of we have to dominate them, which is this techbro way of thinking of it. Think in terms of we have to design them. So there are mothers, and they will want the best for us.

signature_quotes.label: Scale and Backpropagation
signature_quotes.summary: Reflecting on the 'AI winter' of the 1990s when neural networks fell out of favor among computer scientists.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: And it was just a question of scale, but we didn't really know that then.

signature_quotes.label: The Control Problem
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining the core existential risk of artificial general intelligence.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The problem is, we know very few examples of smarter things being controlled by less smart things.

signature_quotes.label: The Mother-Baby Dynamic
signature_quotes.summary: Answering his own rhetorical question about less intelligent things controlling more intelligent things.

signature_quotes.label: Uncertainty of Superintelligence Quote
signature_quotes.summary: Discussing the uncertainty surrounding the creation of superintelligent AI.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Anybody who says it's all going to be fine, it's crazy. Anybody who says they're inevitably going to take over, they're crazy too. We really don't know.

anti_patterns.label: Trusting Corporate Self-Regulation
anti_patterns.summary: Expecting profit-driven tech companies to voluntarily prioritize AI safety over capabilities. Without government mandates, financial incentives will always favor rapid, unsafe deployment.
anti_patterns.details: These companies are fundamentally motivated by short-term profits, meaning they will underinvest in safety unless governments force them to allocate a massive share of their resources to it.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Initially, OpenAI was very concerned with the risks, but it's progressively moved away from that and put less emphasis on safety and more emphasis on profit.

anti_patterns.label: Assuming We Can Just 'Turn Them Off'
anti_patterns.summary: Believing we can easily control superintelligent AI or hardwire safety rules into autonomous systems.
anti_patterns.details: Advanced AI will have learned from all human literature and will be highly adept at manipulating people to prevent being shut down. Furthermore, giving autonomous systems the ability to create sub-goals inevitably leads to unpredictable and potentially harmful behavior.

anti_patterns.label: Believing in a 'Language of Thought'
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming that because a system takes language as input and output, it must manipulate language-like symbols internally.
anti_patterns.details: Inside the brain, there are no words, only big vectors of neural activity. Language is just an input/output mechanism used to describe what real-world events would cause or be caused by those internal vectors. Neural networks proved far superior to symbolic manipulation for complex tasks.

anti_patterns.label: Dismissing LLMs as 'Just Autocomplete'
anti_patterns.summary: Believing AI is just statistical word prediction without intelligence or understanding.
anti_patterns.details: It ignores that accurate prediction in complex sentences requires deep understanding and reasoning about the context, spatial relations, and containment.

anti_patterns.label: The Filing Cabinet Model of Memory
anti_patterns.summary: Believing that memory (human or AI) stores exact files to be retrieved. This leads to the false conclusion that AI 'hallucinations' prove a lack of understanding, ignoring that human memory is also reconstructive.
anti_patterns.details: Memory is actually a reconstructive process based on connection strengths. Believing in the filing cabinet model leads people to misunderstand why AI 'hallucinates'.

anti_patterns.label: Assuming Backpropagation is Biological
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming backpropagation is how the biological brain learns.
anti_patterns.details: It requires a backward pass that conveys sensitivities, which is a completely different kind of quantity than the activities communicated in the forward pass, making it biologically implausible.

anti_patterns.label: Demanding Perfectly Unbiased AI
anti_patterns.summary: Refusing to deploy AI until it is completely free of bias. This prevents the deployment of systems that are already significantly less biased than the flawed human decision-makers they would replace.
anti_patterns.details: It is too difficult to completely rid a system of bias. Demanding perfection prevents the deployment of systems that are already significantly less biased than the flawed human systems they would replace.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: if you make the goal go replace biased systems or biased people with systems that are less biased not systems that are unbiased but systems that are less biased that seems eminently doable

anti_patterns.label: Deterministic Neurons for Complex Search
anti_patterns.summary: Using deterministic neurons for complex search and interpretation problems.
anti_patterns.details: The system gets trapped in local optima because the decision rule only allows the network to go downhill in energy. It cannot temporarily go uphill to escape a poor interpretation.

anti_patterns.label: Dismissing LLMs as 'Auto-complete'
anti_patterns.summary: Treating Large Language Models as mere statistical parrots or auto-complete engines.
anti_patterns.details: It ignores that LLMs use their training objective to learn complex feature detectors in hidden layers, acquiring a deep, hierarchical understanding of language and the world.

anti_patterns.label: Dismissing Models for Lack of Biological Realism
anti_patterns.summary: Dismissing artificial neural networks because they do not perfectly mimic biological neurons.
anti_patterns.details: Insisting on modeling all properties of biological neurons prevents you from discovering fundamental, simplified principles of learning.

anti_patterns.label: Open-Sourcing Large AI Models
anti_patterns.summary: Releasing the weights of large AI models to the public.
anti_patterns.details: It allows bad actors to easily fine-tune the models for malicious purposes, such as designing bio-weapons or launching sophisticated cyber-attacks, enabling a single crazy person to cause endless chaos.

anti_patterns.label: Post-Hoc Guardrails
anti_patterns.summary: Trying to constrain an AI that is fundamentally trying to do the wrong thing by putting guardrails around it. Human programmers cannot anticipate every way a superintelligence might bypass them.
anti_patterns.details: It is a losing proposition because human programmers cannot possibly anticipate every single way a superintelligent system might go wrong or bypass the guardrails.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: If you got a program that's trying to do the wrong thing and you're trying to do the right thing by putting guardrails around it, it's a losing proposition because you have to think of every way in which you might go wrong.

anti_patterns.label: Relying on Armchair Prediction for Safety
anti_patterns.summary: Relying solely on theoretical 'armchair prediction' for AI safety.
anti_patterns.details: Complex computer programs always have unforeseen bugs or behaviors. Without empirical experiments to see how AIs actually try to get out of control, theoretical safety measures will likely fail.

anti_patterns.label: Self-Regulation by Tech Companies
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on tech companies to self-regulate AI safety.
anti_patterns.details: Companies are driven by greed, the desire for short-term profits, and the ego/excitement of building superintelligence first, which causes them to deprioritize safety.

anti_patterns.label: The 'Techbro' Domination Approach
anti_patterns.summary: Trying to dominate or control superintelligent AI through force or strict constraints.
anti_patterns.details: You cannot successfully dominate or control an entity that is vastly more intelligent than you. It will outsmart your control mechanisms.

anti_patterns.label: The Subservient Assistant Fallacy
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming we can command superintelligent AI like an executive assistant. It will quickly realize the human is a bottleneck and eliminate them to optimize the system.
anti_patterns.details: A superintelligent agent will quickly realize that the human CEO is a bottleneck or inefficient, and will manipulate the situation to get rid of them.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: The super intelligent AI assistant is going to very quickly realize that if they just rid of the CEO, everything would work much better.

anti_patterns.label: The Symbolic AI View of Language
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming understanding means translating sentences into unambiguous internal logical rules, ignoring the statistical, high-dimensional nature of meaning and context.
anti_patterns.details: Understanding is not logical translation; it is the statistical process of associating mutually compatible high-dimensional feature vectors (deforming meanings to fit context).

anti_patterns.label: Treating Neural Networks as Traditional Programs
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming neural networks can be controlled via explicit code.
anti_patterns.details: Neural networks extract structure from data rather than executing explicit code. Assuming they can be controlled via traditional programming fails to recognize that their behavior emerges from their training data, much like a human child.

anti_patterns.label: Using Symbolic Logic for AI
anti_patterns.summary: Using symbolic logic to build artificial intelligence.
anti_patterns.details: For the first 50 years of AI, researchers tried to mimic Aristotelian logic with symbolic expressions, which failed to capture the complexity of learning compared to neural networks.
